spotlight: The aftermath of the Gulf war dominates today: the IEA warns of fresh oil price spikes, Germany fears energy rationing, and the UN flags a looming food crisis. In Beijing, Xi told Trump that Taiwan is the make-or-break point of US-China relations. At home, Havlíček weighs cheaper electricity while Schillerová launches a new retail bond.

stories.title: IEA warns of further oil price spikes as inventories drain

body.text: The International Energy Agency is warning of further 'price spikes' as global oil inventories fall at a record pace in the wake of the Gulf conflict
. The supply squeeze is rippling across the energy chain: Germany is now bracing for its first possible energy rationing this summer because of unstable gas deliveries after the war in the Gulf
. Turkey's central bank has had to lift its inflation target as the same war adds to economic pain
. The cumulative effect is squeezing Europe's heavy industry, with the chemicals sector pushed to breaking point by high energy prices, Chinese competition and the Gulf fallout

shortBody.text: The IEA warns of further oil price spikes as inventories drain at a record pace
. Germany fears summer energy rationing
, Turkey lifted its inflation target
and Europe's chemicals industry is at breaking point

stories.title: Gulf war drives UN food crisis warning

body.text: The conflict in the Persian Gulf has disrupted supplies of fertiliser and energy, and the UN is now warning that millions of people could be left without food
. According to HN, Russia and the United States are profiting from the resulting rise in food prices

. The disruption to fertiliser flows threatens harvests across vulnerable regions, compounding the energy shock already rattling global markets and feeding directly into the inflationary pressure that the same war has unleashed.

shortBody.text: The Gulf war disrupted fertiliser and energy supplies, and the UN warns millions could go without food
; Russia and the US benefit from higher prices

stories.title: Havlíček weighs cheaper electricity, won't cut diesel tax

body.text: Deputy PM Karel Havlíček (ANO) says he does not intend to cut the excise duty on diesel, but is considering relief in the regulated component of the electricity price

. In a separate interview, Havlíček, as industry minister, set out how the government is searching for a mechanism to secure enough gas for next winter, also touching on the nationalisation of ČEZ and a US LNG contract
. The dual focus on power prices and gas storage underlines how the energy file has moved to the centre of Czech economic policy as winter supply risks loom.

shortBody.text: Havlíček won't cut diesel excise duty but is weighing relief on the regulated electricity price
; he also outlined plans to secure gas for next winter, ČEZ nationalisation and a US LNG contract

stories.title: Schillerová launches new Republic Bonds for retail savers

body.text: Finance Minister Schillerová has presented a new issue of Republic Bonds aimed at retail investors, letting people once again lend money to the state
. The move revives a familiar instrument for tapping household savings to help finance the state's borrowing needs, offering ordinary Czechs a direct way to park their money in government debt.

shortBody.text: Schillerová unveiled a new issue of Republic Bonds for retail investors, reviving direct state borrowing from households

stories.title: Transport contracts worth up to CZK 19bn start to flow

body.text: After the president signed the budget law, a wave of contract signings is getting under way at road agency ŘSD and the Railway Administration, with deals worth up to CZK 19bn

. The unlocking of funds marks an unusually busy stretch for infrastructure procurement. The context is mixed elsewhere, however: an audit by the NKÚ found that more than half of subsidies from the Ministry for Regional Development for repairing municipal roads went to insignificant communications
, a separate finding that raises questions about how road spending is targeted.

shortBody.text: After the budget signing, ŘSD and the Railway Administration are signing transport contracts worth up to CZK 19bn
. An NKÚ audit separately found over half of road-repair subsidies went to insignificant roads

stories.title: Italy's U-Power buys Cerva Group to create CZK 12bn firm

body.text: Italian workwear maker U-Power has completed its acquisition of the Czech company Cerva Group, creating a business with combined revenue of more than CZK 12bn

. The deal consolidates the protective-clothing market across the region and gives the Italian buyer a substantial Czech footprint, marking one of the larger cross-border industrial tie-ups of the year.

shortBody.text: Italy's U-Power completed its acquisition of Czech Cerva Group, forming a firm with revenue over CZK 12bn

stories.title: German layoff wave hits VW, BioNTech and beyond

body.text: A wave of layoffs is rolling across German industry, with Volkswagen, BioNTech and other companies announcing job cuts, and analysts fearing this is only the beginning

. The cuts point to deepening strain in Germany's industrial core, where energy costs and structural pressures are weighing on even flagship firms. The bleak corporate mood echoes Honda's first-ever annual loss after it pulled back from electric vehicles
, a sign of how carmakers worldwide are recalibrating strategy.

shortBody.text: VW, BioNTech and other German firms are announcing layoffs, with analysts warning of more to come
. Honda separately posted its first-ever annual loss after retreating from EVs

stories.title: Xi warns Trump that Taiwan is the make-or-break issue

body.text: At the opening of the Beijing summit, Xi Jinping warned Trump that the Taiwan question is the critical point of US-China relations
. Trump's own plan to discuss US arms sales to Taiwan with Xi has rattled Asian allies
. The contrast in style was telling: an NYT analysis notes Trump was flattering while Xi was resolute, a difference that 'spoke volumes'
. For Washington's partners in the region, the prospect of Taiwan's defence becoming a bargaining chip in a leaders' summit is the central worry.

shortBody.text: Xi warned Trump in Beijing that Taiwan is the make-or-break point of US-China ties
; Trump's plan to discuss Taiwan arms sales rattled Asian allies
. NYT noted Trump flattered while Xi stayed resolute

stories.title: Gulf states press Trump to leave Iran militarily crippled

body.text: Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Bahrain and Qatar want the US-Israeli operation to end with Iran militarily weakened
. Behind the scenes, the NYT reports that Saudi Arabia and the UAE carried out secret attacks inside Iran, according to US officials
. Yet Riyadh is hedging: the FT reports the Saudis are floating a non-aggression pact with Tehran in response to the US-Israeli action
. The twin tracks — covert strikes and diplomatic overtures — show the Gulf monarchies trying to shape the war's outcome while limiting their own exposure.

shortBody.text: Gulf monarchies want the US-Israeli operation to leave Iran militarily crippled
; the NYT says Saudi Arabia and the UAE carried out secret attacks in Iran
, while the Saudis float a non-aggression pact with Tehran

stories.title: US scraps Poland troop deployment, alarming Europe

body.text: The Pentagon has abruptly cancelled the planned deployment of 4,000 US troops to Poland, a move that has caused unease across Europe
. The reversal lands as NATO foreign ministers gather in Sweden, where all eyes are on Marco Rubio amid questions about the US position in the alliance
. The combination of a withdrawn deployment and uncertainty over American commitment is fuelling fresh anxiety about the durability of US security guarantees on the continent.

shortBody.text: The Pentagon abruptly cancelled a planned deployment of 4,000 US troops to Poland, alarming Europe
. NATO foreign ministers meet in Sweden with all eyes on Rubio

stories.title: Ukraine braces for Russian spring offensive, hits back with drones

body.text: Analysts warn that once the weather turns, Russia will resume its offensive, while Ukraine is meanwhile sending hundreds of drones deep into Russian territory daily, hitting even the Primorsk oil terminal
. Kyiv is also trying to reduce its dependence on US technology: arms maker Fire Point is launching its own satellites
. On the diplomatic front, both Putin and Zelenskyy are cool on the prospect of reviving US-led peace talks, even after the Middle East war ends

shortBody.text: Analysts warn of a Russian spring offensive while Ukraine hits deep targets with drones, including the Primorsk oil terminal
. Kyiv's Fire Point is launching its own satellites to cut US reliance
, and Putin and Zelenskyy are both cool on US peace talks

stories.title: Plaga concedes school reform stalled, modernisation in doubt

body.text: New Education Minister Plaga (ANO) admits that the radical pruning of secondary school fields of study did not work out and the reform is being postponed by a year
. Separately, the ministry has presented a reform of higher education that would weaken academic senates, strengthen the powers of rectors and create a new university council

. The two strands together signal an ambitious but bumpy attempt to overhaul Czech education from secondary schools to universities, with the question now whether Plaga can deliver or will again be left with promises.

shortBody.text: Plaga concedes the radical pruning of secondary-school fields failed and reform is delayed a year
. The ministry separately proposed weakening academic senates and strengthening rectors via a new university council

stories.title: Munich elects first Green mayor, ending 42 years of SPD rule

body.text: For the first time, Munich has a mayor from the Greens: 35-year-old Dominik Krause defeated Social Democrat Reiter, ending 42 years of uninterrupted SPD rule in the city

. The result marks a generational and political shift in one of Germany's largest cities, where the SPD had long appeared unassailable. Krause's win gives the Greens a high-profile municipal showcase at a time of broader uncertainty in German politics.

shortBody.text: The Greens' Dominik Krause, 35, became Munich's first Green mayor, ending 42 years of SPD rule by beating Reiter

stories.title: Slovak institutions rocked by string of scandals

body.text: Denník N reports the new head of Slovakia's caves stole Czech colleagues' backpacks and left them at the mercy of the freezing cold, a case shaking the institution
. In a separate scandal, bodies of the deceased at a Prešov hospital were stored on shelves alongside planks and boxes
. And an investigation into a hacienda linked to Bödör found that its first guest was the owner's sister from the neighbouring village, who paid EUR 1,000 for two nights

shortBody.text: The new head of Slovakia's caves allegedly stole Czech colleagues' backpacks and left them in the freezing cold
; a Prešov hospital stored bodies on shelves with planks and boxes
; and a Bödör-linked hacienda's first guest was the owner's sister, paying EUR 1,000

stories.title: Bank results again a refuge in turbulent times
stories.author: Karel Nedvěd

body.text: According to Fio banka analyst Karel Nedvěd, the solid fourth-quarter 2025 results from Erste, Komerční banka and Moneta once again did not disappoint

. He frames the strong banking numbers as a point of certainty in an otherwise turbulent period, with the sector continuing to deliver reliable earnings even as geopolitical and inflationary risks cloud the wider outlook.

shortBody.text: Nedvěd argues Erste, Komerční banka and Moneta again delivered solid Q4 2025 results, a point of certainty in turbulent times

stories.title: Are we facing another inflation jump?
stories.author: Julie Hrstková

body.text: Julie Hrstková warns of the risks of a fresh inflation spike in connection with rising oil prices following the war in the Persian Gulf

. She casts it as a possible 'return to the past', drawing a line from the energy shock now coursing through markets to the inflationary episodes households thought were behind them, and questioning how prepared policymakers are for a renewed price surge.

shortBody.text: Hrstková warns of a possible new inflation spike driven by rising oil prices after the Gulf war, a 'return to the past'

stories.title: Czech economy grew three times faster than Slovakia's
stories.author: Anna Šuhajdová

body.text: Anna Šuhajdová compares last year's growth in the Czech Republic at 2.6 percent with Slovakia's 0.8 percent, noting the Czech economy grew three times faster

. And, she argues, this year will be no different — the divergence between the two neighbouring economies is set to deepen. The widening gap, she suggests, reflects structural differences that will keep pulling the two former federation partners apart.

shortBody.text: Šuhajdová notes the Czech economy grew 2.6% versus Slovakia's 0.8% and argues the divergence will deepen this year

stories.title: Why office construction has stalled in Prague
stories.author: Pavel Kadera

body.text: Pavel Kadera writes that Prague's office market is seeing a slump in new construction, driven by two trends

. Developers are being lured toward residential construction, while offices only pay off in the most central locations. The result is a reshaping of the capital's development pipeline, as builders chase housing demand and treat new office projects as worthwhile only where premium central sites justify the risk.

shortBody.text: Kadera explains Prague's office construction slump: developers prefer residential projects and offices only pay off in central locations

stories.title: Are fears over corporate and private debt justified?
stories.author: Jiří Król

body.text: Jiří Król argues that the debate over the valuation of private credit portfolios and the impact of AI on the software sector is exaggerated

. His reassurance lands against a more cautious backdrop in the German press: a FAZ analysis asks how dangerous the flood of private credit really is, pointing to rising withdrawals from funds, valuation questions and worries about AI's effect on software companies
. The two pieces frame the same debate from opposite ends of the optimism scale.

shortBody.text: Król argues fears over private-credit valuations and AI's impact on software are exaggerated
. FAZ takes a more cautious view, citing rising fund withdrawals and valuation questions

stories.title: Investment bubbles are a normal feature of markets
stories.author: Michal Stupavský

body.text: Conseq investment strategist Michal Stupavský writes about theories of investment bubbles drawing on Grantham, Shiller, Soros and Buffett, and the tendency of valuations to revert to the mean

. He treats bubbles as a recurring, almost ordinary feature of financial markets rather than an aberration, urging investors to recognise the pattern and to expect stretched valuations eventually to return toward their long-run averages.

shortBody.text: Stupavský discusses bubble theories from Grantham, Shiller, Soros and Buffett, framing bubbles as a normal market feature with valuations reverting to the mean

stories.title: How Britain locked itself in a debt cage

body.text: An FT Big Read offers a deep analysis of how Britain ended up trapped in a structure of high public debt after decades of neglected reforms

. The piece traces the accumulation of fiscal constraints that now limit the country's room for manoeuvre, portraying a 'debt cage' built up over years of postponed decisions rather than any single crisis.

shortBody.text: An FT Big Read analyses how decades of neglected reforms left Britain trapped in a high-debt 'cage'
